title: Same Pre-training Loss, Better Downstream: Implicit Bias Matters for Language Models

structured.summary: The paper challenges the assumption that pre-training loss fully explains downstream performance in language models, introducing flatness as a key factor. Through empirical experiments and theoretical analysis, it argues that implicit bias in training algorithms affects the choice of model minima, impacting transferability.

strengths.point: Introduction of flatness as a factor.
strengths.evidence: The paper presents flatness as a correlating factor with downstream performance, challenging the sole reliance on pre-training loss.
Experiments demonstrate that models with identical pre-training loss can perform differently in downstream tasks, highlighting implicit bias.
The paper provides a theoretical basis showing that SGD prefers flatter minima, correlated with better downstream performance.

weaknesses.evidence: The claim that pre-training loss does not fully correlate with downstream task performance has been shown in previous studies like Saunshi et al., 2022.
Experiments are conducted on simplified datasets, as mentioned in Section 2.2, which may not represent real-world complexity.
Theoretical analysis primarily adapts existing tools from supervised learning without novel theoretical insights.
The paper emphasizes flatness as correlated with performance but doesn't integrate other factors, as seen in Sections 4 and 5.
The results are focused on specific conditions, lacking exploration of different architectures or datasets (Section 2.2).
Terms like 'saturation regime' are introduced without rigorous mathematical definitions in Section 2.2.
Detailed experimental setups, hyperparameters, and datasets are not well-documented, as noted in Section 2.2.
The paper references works like Damian et al., 2021, but doesn't thoroughly critique or compare them.
The claimed contributions in the abstract are not fully differentiated from prior works.
The discussion remains conceptual without applying insights to practical language modeling scenarios.
The paper assumes the trace of the Hessian is the most appropriate metric for flatness, lacking stronger justification.
Theoretical proofs are demonstrated in a synthetic setting, potentially limiting generalizability to broader contexts.

questions.question: How well do these findings extend to real-world large datasets?
questions.motivation: The experiments are conducted on simplified datasets, so generalizability to larger models is crucial for practical application.

questions.question: Could other factors besides flatness influence downstream performance?
questions.motivation: Focusing heavily on flatness may overlook other influential factors in language modeling.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of novelty in findings.. The claim that pre-training loss does not fully correlate with downstream task performance has been shown in previous studies like Saunshi et al., 2022
critique_points.point_002: Limited experimental scope.. Experiments are conducted on simplified datasets, as mentioned in Section 2.2, which may not represent real-world complexity
critique_points.point_003: Insufficient theoretical contribution.. Theoretical analysis primarily adapts existing tools from supervised learning without novel theoretical insights
critique_points.point_004: Over-reliance on flatness.. The paper emphasizes flatness as correlated with performance but doesn't integrate other factors, as seen in Sections 4 and 5
critique_points.point_005: Incompleteness of empirical evidence.. The results are focused on specific conditions, lacking exploration of different architectures or datasets (Section 2.2)
critique_points.point_006: Ambiguous definition of terms.. Terms like 'saturation regime' are introduced without rigorous mathematical definitions in Section 2.2
critique_points.point_007: Reproducibility concerns.. Detailed experimental setups, hyperparameters, and datasets are not well-documented, as noted in Section 2.2
critique_points.point_008: Insufficient engagement with existing literature.. The paper references works like Damian et al., 2021, but doesn't thoroughly critique or compare them
critique_points.point_009: Overstatement of contributions.. The claimed contributions in the abstract are not fully differentiated from prior works
critique_points.point_010: Lack of clear practical implications.. The discussion remains conceptual without applying insights to practical language modeling scenarios
critique_points.point_011: Flatness metric measurement.. The paper assumes the trace of the Hessian is the most appropriate metric for flatness, lacking stronger justification
critique_points.point_012: Limited scope in theoretical proofs.. Theoretical proofs are demonstrated in a synthetic setting, potentially limiting generalizability to broader contexts
